title: What LeNet Could and Couldn't Do
concept: LeNet was strong at compact grayscale digit recognition, but its small scale and early-era limits meant it was not built for the larger, noisier, more varied vision problems tackled by later CNNs.

exercises.prompt: Which job sounds closer to LeNet's comfort zone?
exercises.explain: Correct — LeNet's sweet spot was small grayscale digits, not giant complex scenes.

options.text: Explaining every object in a wildlife documentary frame
Driving a car through a rainy city by itself

cards.title: Great in its lane
cards.body: LeNet is easiest to understand when you judge it against the task it was built for. It was strong for small handwritten digits, not a universal solution for every vision problem.

cards.title: Its strengths and limits
cards.body: LeNet handled small grayscale inputs and 10-way digit classification well. But compared with modern models, it used less data, less compute, shallower stacks, and a much narrower problem setting.

cards.title: A bike is not a failure because it is not a spaceship
cards.body: A bicycle is excellent for a short ride even though it cannot cross the solar system. In the same way, LeNet succeeded at its intended route without needing to solve giant color-scene recognition problems.

cards.title: Digits vs. natural photos
cards.body: Classifying a centered grayscale 8 is very different from identifying dogs, trucks, trees, shadows, and backgrounds in a big color photo. Later models had to handle more classes, more variation, and much bigger datasets.

cards.title: Limited does not mean unimportant
cards.body: Sometimes students hear that an older model had limits and assume it was not useful. That is the wrong lesson. LeNet was powerful for its era and task, and its limits help explain why later architectures had to grow.

cards.title: LeNet's sweet spot
cards.body: Its design fit one kind of problem much better than others.

left.text:
Small grayscale digits
Simple backgrounds
Limited class set

right.text:
Large color scenes
Many object types
Far more scale and clutter

exercises.prompt: Which task is LeNet more naturally matched to?
exercises.explain: Exactly — LeNet's design fits compact digit classification far better than messy large-scene understanding.

options.text: Recognizing handwritten digits in small grayscale crops
Detecting many object categories in large color vacation photos
Writing a movie review from memory
Simulating weather for an entire planet

exercises.prompt: True or false: LeNet's limits help explain why later CNN families became deeper, wider, and more data-hungry.
exercises.explain: True — later architectures expanded because new tasks were larger and more varied than LeNet's digit setting.
Grayscale is the key word — LeNet's classic digit tasks did not need rich color-scene reasoning.
exercises.sentence: LeNet was famous for small ___ digit images rather than giant full-color scenes.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best judges LeNet fairly?
exercises.explain: Right — a fair judgment keeps both truths together: LeNet was effective for its task, and later tasks demanded bigger models.

options.text: It was strong for small digit recognition, but later vision problems needed more scale, data, and complexity
It failed because any model that is not modern must be useless
It could already solve every future vision challenge, so later CNNs added nothing important

exercises.prompt: A student says, 'If LeNet wasn't built for giant modern image datasets, why celebrate it at all?' Which answer is best?
exercises.explain: Exactly. LeNet should be celebrated for doing its intended job well and for showing how CNN design could work. Its limits are part of the story because they explain why later architectures kept evolving.

options.text: Because it succeeded on an important early task and revealed the strengths and scaling limits that later CNNs would build on
Because a good architecture never has a task boundary or design tradeoff
Because modern vision problems are actually easier than digit recognition
Because LeNet proved color scenes do not need bigger models or datasets
